channel.title: Deployments on Dijure | Digital Architectures with a hint of Java
channel.description: Recent content in Deployments on Dijure | Digital Architectures with a hint of Java
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 by Dijure LLC · All rights reserved · Some design elements by Themefisher. Select images from Unsplash. Created using Hugo.

item.title: Deployments
item.description:
This lab introduces you to Deployments and explores some important Deployment usage techniques and features. The instructions in this lab closely follow the text of Chapter 10, “Deployments,” in the book Kubernetes: Up and Running, third edition.

In this lab, you will learn how to:

    ☐ Create, read, update, and delete a Deployment
    ☐ Inspect a Deployment
    ☐ Scale a Deployment

If you aren’t familiar with the basics of Pods and ReplicaSets, then explore these labs first:

item.title: Application Run and Update
item.description:
To prepare or review for this challenge, check out the Kubernetes Fundamentals: First Kubernetes Application lab first.

This is a skill assessment lab to help strengthen your understanding of the basics of running an application on Kubernetes. Running an application is one of the first Kubernetes skills to master, and you should be comfortable using the command line tool kubectl to carry out these fundamental commands.

In this challenge, you will use kubectl to rapidly deploy, start, expose, invoke, and update an application—all from the command line.

item.title: Assembling a Multi-Component Application
item.description:
This is a skill assessment lab to help strengthen your understanding of deploying basic applications on Kubernetes.

Dogs race sleds across Alaska. Cats walk themselves. Both need and give love. How cool is that? Between a dog and a cat, what would you choose as your preferred companion?

You will assemble a voting application where others can vote on that question. The voting application is a classic Hello World example using multiple containers. Getting all of these components to run in concert on Kubernetes is a solid way to test your skills.

item.title: Configuration Conundrum
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that is not displaying any configuration data. Your goal is to find the problem and modify the deployment so the application can properly access its configuration.

item.title: Consul Mesh
item.description:
This lab provides an ideal place to start using the HashiCorp Consul service mesh on Kubernetes.

HashiCorp Consul solves the challenges that organizations of all sizes encounter with microservices architectures. This ranges from operating in various distributed environments and geographical locations, to meeting the need of securing all application traffic. The world is rapidly changing and evolving, so is the computing networking layer.

Consul is a service mesh solution providing a full featured control plane with service discovery, configuration, and segmentation functionality. Each of these features can be used individually as needed, or they can be used together to build a full service mesh. Consul requires a data plane and supports both a proxy and native integration model. Consul ships with a simple built-in proxy so that everything works out of the box, but also supports 3rd party proxy integrations such as Envoy.

item.title: Deploy and Run a Multicontainer Application
item.description:
This is a skill assessment lab to help strengthen your understanding how modular microservices applications are assembled on Kubernetes.

Weaveworks provides a nice example of several pre-written microservices that are coupled to some common data stores. Without writing that actual code, you can pull these containers together into a functional app complete with a web interface, microservices, and datastores. Each application feature is appropriately modularized into microservices and packaged into their respective containers. These microservices are back with some datastores, and there is even a RabbitMQ messaging service used for async communication between some services.

item.title: Exploring Istio
item.description:
This lab provides a sandbox to explore features in the Istio service mesh.

… Service Mesh, a dedicated infrastructure layer to run fast, reliable and secure network of microservices, container orchestration systems to provide a higher level of deployment infrastructure abstraction, and evolution of continuous delivery systems [sic] to build, test and deploy microservices as containers. – Zhamak Dehghani

Before the sandbox is fully ready, there are a few steps needed to complete the sandbox configuration:

item.title: False Positive News
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. This particular lab focuses on one of the most subtle yet critical aspects of Kubernetes application deployment: health check probes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster and a tech news sentiment analysis service that appears to be running correctly. However, despite all components showing as Running, the application fails to deliver results to users. Your mission is to investigate why the health reporting is giving false positives and fix the underlying probe configurations.

item.title: First Kubernetes Application
item.description:
This lab takes you through the basics of deploying an application on Kubernetes. The application is nothing notable, but it’s simple enough to get started.

In this lab, you will learn how to:

    ☐ Deploy and start the echoserver application
    ☐ Scale the application
    ☐ Use Services to load balance between multiple Pods of the app
    ☐ Leverage Kubernetes resilience to maintain the declared state even with failures
    ☐ Rollout a new version of an application

item.title: Headed for Trouble
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that’s experiencing an issue with message ordering. Your goal is to diagnose the problem and configure the system correctly to ensure the application works as expected.

item.title: Ingress to Canary Deployment
item.description:
This is a skill assessment lab to help strengthen your understanding of discovering, exposing, and accessing services in a Kubernetes cluster.

Service access starts with exposing a group of Pods with a Kubernetes Service. That Service is given a name, a virtual IP address, and a port. From there, that service can be exposed and accessed via simple port-forwarding or more complex ingress rules. In this challenge, you will start with some simple access, then work your way up to public access using a URL with ingress rules.

item.title: Istio
item.description:
Networking layers have traditionally been a burden on engineering organizations to get networking right. Networking layers such as meshes look as clusters as a whole and attempt to add networking controls. An efficient mesh is one that can be declared with infrastructure as code ( IaC ).

Istio is a specific networking mesh you can add to your cloud native cluster. It’s tuned for ease of use with Kubernetes, and can be used for meshing other services outside of Kubernetes. Use Istio for declarative control over these networking architecture x-ilities:

item.title: Istio Traffic Management
item.description:
This lab explores seven traffic management features with the Istio service mesh.

… Service Mesh, a dedicated infrastructure layer to run fast, reliable and secure network of microservices, container orchestration systems to provide a higher level of deployment infrastructure abstraction, and evolution of continuous delivery systems [sic] to build, test and deploy microservices as containers. – Zhamak Dehghani

A significant feature in Istio provides you the ability to control traffic of messages between services by submitting rules coded in declarative manifests (YAMLs). With this lab, you can teach yourself these traffic management features by experimenting with each declaration and observing the behaviors.

item.title: NGINX
item.description:
These next steps provide an ideal place to start deploying and running your first application on Kubernetes. With a Kubernetes cluster and a the command line tool kubectl, a few steps will get an NGINX web server running.

In this lab, you will learn how to:

    ☐ Use the basics of the kubectl CLI tool
    ☐ Install NGINX on Kubernetes
    ☐ Deploy containers as Deployments in Pods
    ☐ Use a Service to access a replication of Pods

item.title: No Soup For You
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that is failing to access Kubernetes API resources. Your goal is to diagnose the issue and configure the proper permissions to get the application functioning.

item.title: Resource Roulette
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that is failing for some reason. Your goal is to find the problem get the application to function normally.

item.title: Scaling and Updating an Application
item.description:
This is a skill assessment lab to help strengthen your understanding of starting, accessing, scaling, and updating applications on Kubernetes.

You will be running a cancer detector. The application is a machine learning model already written and tuned to provide probabilities of cancer found in the cell image slides that you provide to the service.

The Tumor Proliferation Assessment Challenge 2016 (TUPAC16) was created to develop state-of-the-art algorithms for automatic prediction of tumor proliferation scores from whole-slide histopathology images of breast tumors. The IBM CODAIT team trained a mitosis detection model (a modified ResNet-50 model) on the TUPAC16 auxiliary mitosis dataset and then applied it to the whole-slide images for predicting tumor proliferation scores.

item.title: Scaling Stalemate
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that isn’t scaling automatically despite increased load. Your goal is to identify and fix the issues preventing the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) from functioning correctly.

item.title: Stay in Your Lane
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster with an admission controller webhook already installed. You will be challenged with a deployment that isn’t behaving as expected. Your goal is to find the problem and get the application to function normally at the desired scale.

item.title: The Communication Breakdown
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster with a multiservice healthcare application. You will be challenged with debugging communication issues between the frontend patient portal and the backend medical results service. Your goal is to find the problems and get the application to function normally.

item.title: The Elusive Application
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that is failing for some reason. Your goal is to find the problem and get the application to function normally.

item.title: The False Start
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that appears to be running but isn’t functioning correctly. Your goal is to find the problem and get the application to function normally.

item.title: The Invisible Barrier
item.description:
This lab is part of a series of labs that challenge your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab present you with a common problems often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an application that is failing for some reason. Your goal is to find the problem get the application to function normally.

item.title: The Perpetual Machine
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when deploying and running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with a data processing job that is misbehaving. Your goal is to find the problem and get the job to function normally.

item.title: The Silent Stats
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. Each lab presents you with a common problem often encountered when running applications on Kubernetes.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You will be challenged with an X-ray analysis application that appears to be running but isn’t functioning correctly. This lab will introduce you to more advanced debugging techniques that go beyond standard log analysis.

item.title: Tiptoe Through the Meanies
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. This particular lab focuses on mysterious application instability in a microservice architecture that seems to have no clear cause.

You are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster and a live satellite tracker application that monitors five popular satellites in real time. The application uses a microservice architecture with separate satellite tracking services and an aggregator frontend. Users report that individual satellites randomly disappear, data fails to load intermittently, and the system shows unpredictable failures. Your mission is to investigate these stability issues and restore reliable service.

item.title: Vanishing Information
item.description:
This lab is part of a series that challenges your skills with running applications on Kubernetes. This particular lab focuses on one of the most critical aspects of Kubernetes storage: persistent volumes and data persistence.

In this lab, you are provided with a working Kubernetes cluster. You also have a telemedicine prescription refill service that appears to be running correctly. However, despite all components showing as Running, patient medication orders vanish every time pods restart. Your mission is to investigate why critical prescription data isn’t persisting and fix the underlying storage configurations.
